retrieved_docs.content: REM is a useful approach when many mutations are desired; however, there is less control over the resulting modifications. This technique has helped map out critical residues of proteins (eg, EcoRV restriction endonuclease). REM can be accomplished through different methods:

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing -- TALENS vs. Zinc Finger Nucleases
retrieved_docs.content: TALENs and ZFN are gene-editing tools with similar principles; however, they have different underlying designs. They both are proteins that consist of DNA binding domains (ie, TALE proteins vs. zinc fingers domains) and DNA cleavage domains (eg, Fok1). DNA specificity resides in the TALE proteins and the zinc finger domains. For cleavage to occur, the restriction enzyme must dimerize; therefore, at least 2 proteins are needed per target DNA. A double-stranded DNA break is made once dimerization occurs at a target location. Similar to CRISPR, this is repaired by NHEJ (with the possibility of insertions and deletions) or homologous recombination (where exogenous DNA can be added). A key difference between TALENs and ZFN is that TALE proteins recognize a single base, while zinc finger domains recognize 3 or 4 bases. Given that zinc fingers must recognize multiple bases, it may reduce DNA specificity compared to TALE proteins. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, G Protein Coupled Receptors -- Fundamentals -- Signaling by GPCRs
retrieved_docs.content: The regulatory subunits of PKA (also known as A-kinase) are important for the sub-cellular localization of PKA, which is facilitated by the interaction of A-kinase anchoring proteins (AKAPs) with the regulatory subunits. One of the well-known targets of PKA is CRE-binding protein (CREB); PKA phosphorylates CREB on a specific serine residue. Phosphorylation of CREB allows the recruitment of a transcriptional co-activator CREB-binding protein (CBP), which stimulates the transcription of various target genes. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Mechanism -- Exogenous
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory techniques : Different laboratory techniques, including PCR, non-PCR, and gene-editing tools, induce mutagenesis. These are discussed further in the "Testing/Methods" section below.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing -- CRISPR-Cas9
retrieved_docs.content: This genome-editing technology is derived from bacterial defenses against viruses and foreign plasmids. It has 2 vital components: a guide RNA (gRNA) that binds in a complementary way to a target DNA sequence and an endonuclease (Cas9) that causes a dsDNA break that requires repair. Cas9-induced site-specific dsDNA breaks induce endogenous cell repair mechanisms, which can be exploited to modify DNA. The error-prone nonhomologous end-joining (NHEJ) can rapidly ligate DNA breaks; however, it may generate small insertions and deletions that can cause frameshift mutations. Alternatively, dsDNA breaks can be repaired by homology-directed repair (HDR), which can insert exogenous DNA and introduce precise genome editing. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Mutagenesis is a technique used in molecular biology to create mutant genes, proteins, and organisms. Two primary mutagenesis techniques are site-directed mutagenesis (SDM) and random-and-extensive mutagenesis (REM). These methods are accomplished mainly by primarily conducted (PCR) and non-polymerase chain reactions (non-PCR). Other mutagenesis performing/Cas9 technology methods are TALENs (transcription activator-like effector nucleases) and Zinc-Finger Nucleases (ZFN).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Editing Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc finger proteins are fused to a DNA cleavage domain to make zinc finger nucleases. These nucleases cause double-stranded breaks at a very specific targeted DNA sequence, which can then disrupt that specific sequence due to errors during the non-homologous repair end-joining pathway, thereby permanently disrupting the targeted gene. The zinc finger nucleases are highly individualized for their respective DNA sequences, and a new one must be engineered for each specific site, making this process too laborious and expensive. [3] Transcription Activator-Like Effectors (TALE)

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The following exergonic reaction catalyzed by PEP carboxykinase (PEPCK), a lyase, uses GTP as a phosphate donor to phosphorylate oxaloacetate and form PEP. Glucocorticoids induce PEPCK gene expression; cortisol, after binding to its steroid receptor intracellularly, moves inside the cell nucleus. Then, the zinc finger domain in cortisol binds to the glucocorticoid response element (GRE) on DNA. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing -- Site-directed Mutagenesis
retrieved_docs.content: SDM is a technique where DNA can be modified at a specific nucleotide location, causing a predetermined amino acid change. These substitution mutations can result in drastic changes in protein conformation and function. This technique requires (1) a DNA template with a target gene, (2) knowledge of the target gene's nucleotide sequence, and (3) a short primer (commonly 20 to 30 base pairs) complementary to the target sequence that is modified to contain a mismatched nucleotide (typically 1 to 3 base pairs that cause an amino acid change). The general procedure for SDM is the following: Step 1: Separation of the 2 strands of template DNA. This can be accomplished by heat or an alkali solution. Step 2: Addition of the modified DNA primer. Once the DNA primer anneals with a single strand, a DNA polymerase replicates the strand. Step 3: The second round of replication yields a mutant DNA strand that can be used to synthesize a modified protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Fundamentals -- Regulation of Gluconeogenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple factors contribute to the regulation of substrates, enzymes, and reactions involved in gluconeogenesis, including: Glucagon regulates gluconeogenesis through [10] Changes in allosteric regulators (reduces the levels of fructose-2,6 bisphosphate) Covalent modification of enzyme activity (phosphorylation of pyruvate kinase results in its inactivation) Induction of enzymes gene expression (glucagon via CRE response elements increases the expression of PEPCK Acetyl CoA activates pyruvate carboxylase allosterically [11] Substrate availability might increase or decrease the rate of gluconeogenesis AMP inhibits fructose-1,6 bisphosphatase allosterically [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Transthyretin Amyloid Cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: A gene-editing approach using CRISPR-associated protein 9 is under development to treat hATTR. This approach hypothesizes that target DNA can be permanently modified. Thus, permanently silencing mutated TTR may treat hATTR. PRX-004 is being studied to treat ATTR amyloidosis. It is hypothesized to work by removing ATTR deposits from the myocardium.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Editing Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: Gene editing involves directly altering the sequence of a gene. All epigenetic modification techniques must contain a DNA binding molecule and an active epigenetic modification molecule. These modifications first require DNA to be cut using an endonuclease to make a double-stranded break and then allow the two ends of the DNA to repair. During this repair process, the DNA in the vicinity of the cut undergoes replacement by a new sequence using a template delivered into the target cell along with an endonuclease. Three of the most widely studied DNA binding molecules are zinc finger proteins (ZFPs), transcription activator-like effectors (TALEs), and deactivated Cas9 (dCas9). [1] Zinc Finger Proteins (ZFP)

Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect fibrotic stiffening. Focal choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) illustrate localized tissue loss and scarring that may involve both choroid and retina. The choroidea serves as the anatomic substrate for additional inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative and metastatic processes, including the entities listed above, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dystrophy of the choroid, and choroidal tears. Massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) exemplifies the choroidea’s role as a site for hemorrhagic events. Collectively, these interconnected entities depict a comprehensive view of posterior‑segment ocular

Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.
